prevalence.source: Sociedade Brasileira de Endocrinologia e Metabologia (SBEM) + epidemiological syntheses
prevalence.trend: stable; increased detection with more TSH testing

burden.mortalityRate: Low; increased risk via atrial fibrillation and thyroid storm (rare)

epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Suppressed TSH with elevated free T4 and/or T3; etiologic workup with TRAb, thyroid uptake/scan and ultrasound as indicated; aligned with SBEM/ATA guidance

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no routine screening; TSH testing is common in symptomatic patients and high-risk groups
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good symptom control with antithyroid drugs when follow-up is consistent; relapse common in Graves

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: TSH/T4 available in SUS; uptake scans and endocrinology referrals more limited outside major cities
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Methimazole (preferred) or PTU in specific cases; beta-blockers for symptom control; radioactive iodine and surgery in selected cases
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Specialist access and variable availability of definitive therapy pathways (radioiodine/surgery) across regions
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Core lab tests and antithyroid medications available; strong endocrine society guidance
